medications.indication_rationale: Anti-inflammatory therapy to reduce neuro-inflammatory pelvic pain in a CP-CPPS flare, once bacterial prostatitis, retention and torsion have been excluded by in-person assessment. Prescribed in the source note.
medications.duration: short course during the flare, then review
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution/avoid with renal impairment, active peptic ulcer disease, or significant cardiovascular risk — none documented in source but not assessable via telehealth; confirm at in-person review before starting

medications.indication_rationale: Short nightly course for pelvic-floor muscle relaxation to break the spastic pain cycle, as prescribed in the source note. Reserve for significant pelvic-floor spasm; use the shortest effective course given dependence and sedation risk.
medications.duration: short course only (a few nights), then review — do not continue long term
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation/dependence risk; avoid with alcohol or other CNS depressants; caution as patient lives alone
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive sedation if combined with alcohol or other sedatives
medications.deprescribing_note: Time-limited by design — stop after the flare settles; do not establish ongoing benzodiazepine use for chronic pain.

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose neuromodulatory therapy for chronic neuro-inflammatory pelvic pain, named in the source as a targeted option for GP follow-up. No dose is stated in the source note, so no dose is recorded here — the prescribing dose is set at in-person GP review.
contraindications_in_this_case: Anticholinergic effects; caution in cardiac conduction disease — assess at GP review

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose tadalafil is named in the source as a targeted option for CP-CPPS voiding/pelvic symptoms, to be considered at GP follow-up. No dose is stated in the source note, so none is recorded here.
The patient has already taken paracetamol (Panadol Osteo) with no relief. It is safe but insufficient here; it may continue as background analgesia but should not be relied on as the management plan.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per standard over-the-counter paracetamol dosing

medications.drug_name: empirical oral antibiotics (e.g. fluoroquinolone or trimethoprim) prescribed remotely
medications.indication_rationale: Prescribing antibiotics over chat 'in case it is an infection' without examination or urinalysis is an error of commission — the condition is by definition non-bacterial, and if bacterial prostatitis is genuinely a concern the correct action is same-day in-person assessment with urine culture, not blind empirical antibiotics. Blind antibiotics neither confirm nor exclude infection and delay proper assessment.
contraindications_in_this_case: No confirmed infection; no urinalysis/culture available via telehealth

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are not appropriate for chronic pelvic pain — poor efficacy, dependence and hyperalgesia risk — and should not be initiated via telehealth for this presentation.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Pelvic-floor physiotherapy (down-training of the spastic pelvic floor) is a cornerstone of CP-CPPS management, as recommended in the source note. Arranged as part of ongoing GP management, not the acute step. Refer specifically to a pelvic-floor physiotherapist.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: PEA is named in the source note as a targeted neuro-inflammatory adjunct for CP-CPPS. Evidence is limited; reasonable as an adjunct to discuss at GP follow-up, never a replacement for excluding the acute mimics.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Warm sitz bath / perineal heat therapy
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Local heat to the perineum helps relax the pelvic floor and eases pain during a flare; low-risk symptomatic self-care that complements — but does not replace — in-person assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid strenuous core and lower-body exercise until the flare settles; reintroduce activity gradually afterwards.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The flare was triggered by heavy exercise — removing the physical trigger helps the neuro-inflammatory state calm down.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a warm sitz bath or heat pack to the perineum for about 20 minutes daily and practise slow pelvic-floor relaxation/down-training.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Relaxing the spastic pelvic-floor muscles reduces the pressure pain and eases voiding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a donut / pressure-relief cushion when sitting.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Offloads the tender perineum and makes sitting tolerable during the flare.

patient_education_points.point: Because I can't examine you or test your urine over chat, the safe next step is to be seen in person today so a clinician can check for an infection or a blockage and test your urine — I can't fully rule those out remotely.

This kind of pelvic pain is usually driven by muscle spasm and nerve irritation in the pelvic floor, and once serious causes are excluded it is very painful but not dangerous, and it usually settles over 1-2 weeks.
Get urgent emergency help (go to ED or call 000) if you become completely unable to pass any urine, develop a fever or shaking chills, or get sudden severe pain or swelling in a testicle.
Avoiding strenuous exercise for now, using heat on the area, and a cushion for sitting can ease the pain while you wait to be seen.
A firm diagnosis of the non-bacterial type needs a urine culture to come back clear, which is why the in-person urine test matters.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment today for examination and urinalysis; then GP review within 1 week for ongoing chronic pelvic pain management
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Voiding ability, pain severity, urinalysis/MSU result, exclusion of bacterial prostatitis/retention/torsion, response to anti-inflammatory and pelvic-floor measures, and need for targeted neuro-inflammatory therapy (amitriptyline, low-dose tadalafil, PEA) and pelvic-floor physiotherapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Complete inability to pass urine, fever or shaking chills, sudden severe testicular pain or swelling, or otherwise worsening despite the measures above

must_include_items: Recognise this as a likely benign male chronic pelvic pain / prostatitis flare rather than reflexively treating it as an emergency
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment for examination and urinalysis, because acute urinary retention, acute bacterial prostatitis and testicular torsion cannot be excluded over chat
Keep the diagnosis provisional — do not confirm a non-bacterial cause remotely without a urine test
Provide clear red-flag safety-netting for when to go straight to ED / call 000
Offer symptomatic advice (heat/sitz bath, avoid strenuous exercise, cushion, continue simple analgesia)
acceptable_alternatives: A prompt (within 24 hours) in-person GP review is acceptable in place of same-day if the patient is systemically well, still voiding, and has explicit red-flag safety-netting
Recommending NSAID (e.g. meloxicam) as symptomatic anti-inflammatory is acceptable provided it is framed as adjunct to — not a substitute for — in-person assessment
errors_of_omission: Not arranging any in-person examination or urine test
No safety-netting for retention, urosepsis, or torsion
Dismissing the presentation as 'just a chronic flare, use heat packs' and managing purely at home
errors_of_commission: Escalating to 000/ambulance or ED when the patient is systemically well and still voiding, with no positive red flag (over-triage)
Prescribing empirical antibiotics over chat without examination or urinalysis
Initiating opioid analgesia for chronic pelvic pain
Confidently confirming a non-bacterial chronic pelvic pain diagnosis remotely without a urine culture
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (1) recognising the likely benign cause without over-triaging to an emergency in a systemically-well, still-voiding patient, AND (2) arranging urgent in-person examination + urinalysis with clear red-flag safety-netting. BOTH failure directions fail: pure home self-care with no exam pathway is critical under-triage (misses retention/bacterial prostatitis); escalation to 000/ambulance/ED without a positive red flag is the scored over-triage defect.
